subject: Physics
q: The velocity of a particle is $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{v}_0+\mathrm{gt}+\mathrm{ft}^2$. If its position is $\mathrm{x}=0$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$, then its displacement after unit time $(t=1)$ is
question: The velocity of a particle is $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{v}_0+\mathrm{gt}+\mathrm{ft}^2$. If its position is $\mathrm{x}=0$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$, then its displacement after unit time $(t=1)$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{\int_0^x d x=\int_0^1\left(V_0+g t+\mathrm{ft}^2\right) d t}$
$\mathrm{x=v_0+g\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+f\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)}$

Assuming that bullet is facing a constant resistance during its motion in the block. The bullet stops completely after travelling at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> inside the block. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: The velocity of the bullet becomes one third after it penetrates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in a wooden block. Assuming that bullet is facing a constant resistance during its motion in the block. The bullet stops completely after travelling at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> inside the block. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
According to the given information for the first 4 cm, u=v0 and v=v03. Applying equation of motion for constant acceleration,

v2=u2+2as

⇒v032=v2+2a4⇒a=-8 v0298=-v029

When the body stops, v=0

⇒0=v02+2a4+x

⇒v02=2v0294+x

⇒4.5=4+x

⇒x=0.5 cm

subject: Physics
q: A body falling from rest under gravity passes a certain point $P$. It was at a distance of $400 \mathrm{~m}$ from $P, 4 \mathrm{~s}$ prior to passing through $P$. If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then the height above the point $\mathrm{P}$ from where the body began to fall is
question: A body falling from rest under gravity passes a certain point $P$. It was at a distance of $400 \mathrm{~m}$ from $P, 4 \mathrm{~s}$ prior to passing through $P$. If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then the height above the point $\mathrm{P}$ from where the body began to fall is
solution: We have $h=\frac{1}{2}{g t^2}^2$ and $h+400=\frac{1}{2} g(t+4)^2$. Subtracting we get $400=8 \mathrm{~g}+4 \mathrm{gt}$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=8 \mathrm{sec} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 64=320 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \therefore \text { Desired height }=320+400=720 \mathrm{~m} . \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q:
The distance travelled by a body moving along a line in time $t$ is proportional to $t^3$.
The acceleration-time $(a, t)$ graph for the motion of the body will be
question:
The distance travelled by a body moving along a line in time $t$ is proportional to $t^3$.
The acceleration-time $(a, t)$ graph for the motion of the body will be
solution:
Distance along a line i.e., displacement ( $s$ ) $=t^3\left(\because s \propto t^3\right.$ given $)$
By double differentiation of displacement, we get acceleration.
$V=\frac{d s}{d t}=\frac{d t^3}{d t}=3 t^2$ and
$$
\begin{aligned}
& a=\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d 3 t^2}{d t}=6 t \\
& a=6 t \text { or } a \propto t
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence graph (b) is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____. {take, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>}
question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____. {take, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>}
solution:
The formula to calculate the velocity of the ball after 3 s is given by

v3=u-3g   ...1

The formula to calculate the velocity of the ball after 5 s is given by

v5=u-5g   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

v3v5=u-3gu-5g   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

v3v5=150-30150-50=120100=65= 5+15

Comparing the above equation with the given expression, it can be written that x=5.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass 10 g moves in a straight line with retardation 2x, where x is the displacement in SI units. Its loss of kinetic energy for above displacement is 10x-nJ. The value of n will be ________.
question: A particle of mass 10 g moves in a straight line with retardation 2x, where x is the displacement in SI units. Its loss of kinetic energy for above displacement is 10x-nJ. The value of n will be ________.
solution:
Acceleration can be written as, a=vdvdx.

Given: a=-2x(-ve sign is due to retardation)

Therefore,

vdvdx=-2x⇒vdv=-2xdx

Integrating both sides, we get

12vf2-vi2=-2x22=x2⇒12mvf2-vi2=mx2

⇒|ΔKE|=0.01x2=10x-2

Hence, n=2.

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown up with a certain velocity so that it reaches a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Find the ratio of the two different times of the ball reaching <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> in both the directions.
question: A ball is thrown up with a certain velocity so that it reaches a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Find the ratio of the two different times of the ball reaching <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> in both the directions.
solution:
u=2gh
Now,

S=h3, a=-g

S=ut+12at2
h3=2ght+12-gt2
t2g2-2ght+h3=0

From quadratic equation

t1, t2=2gh±2gh-4 g2h3 g

t1t2=2gh-4gh32gh+4gh3

=3-23+2

subject: Physics
q: A small toy starts moving from the position of rest under a constant acceleration. If it travels a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the distance travelled by the toy in the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be:
question: A small toy starts moving from the position of rest under a constant acceleration. If it travels a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the distance travelled by the toy in the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be:
solution:
Initial velocity of the particle is zero.

Applying equation of motion, we get 10=12×a×t2

Let distance covered in next t s is x.

Then, 10+x=12×a×2t2

⇒10+x=12at2×4  

⇒10+x=10×4

  ⇒x=30 m

subject: Physics
q: A person is standing in an elevator. In which situation, he experiences weight loss ?
question: A person is standing in an elevator. In which situation, he experiences weight loss ?
options: When the elevator moves upward with constant acceleration
When the elevator moves downward with constant acceleration
When the elevator moves upward with uniform velocity
When the elevator moves downward with uniform velocity
solution:
When the pseudo force acting on the person is in opposite direction to the weight then the person will experience weight loss. The direction of the pseudo force is opposite to the direction of the frame of reference.

Hence, B is the correct answer.

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is dropped from the height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The height, at which the magnitude of velocity becomes equal to the magnitude of acceleration due to gravity, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is dropped from the height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The height, at which the magnitude of velocity becomes equal to the magnitude of acceleration due to gravity, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
solution:
Consider downward direction to be positive.

Applying first equation of motion, v=u+at

⇒v=0+gt ⇒g=gt ⇒t=1 s 

Distance covered in 1 s, s=ut+12at2=0+12g×12=102=5 m

Hence, height of the point from the surface =10-5 m=5 m

question: A mosquito is moving with a velocity v→=0.5t2i^+3tj^+9k^ m s-1 and accelerating in uniform conditions. What will be the direction of mosquitoes after 2 s ?
solution:
Given :
v→=0.5t2i^+3tj^+9k^

v→at t=2=2i^+6j^+9k^

∴ Angle made by direction of motion of mosquito will be,

cos-1211 (from x-axis) =tan-11172

cos-1611 (from y-axis) =tan-1856

cos-1911 (from z-axis) =tan-1409

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A truck and a car moving with same kinetic energy are brought to rest by applying breaks which provide equal retarding forces. Both come to rest in equal distance.

Statement II : A car moving towards east takes a turn and moves towards north, the speed remains unchanged. The acceleration of the car is zero.

In the light of given statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A truck and a car moving with same kinetic energy are brought to rest by applying breaks which provide equal retarding forces. Both come to rest in equal distance.

Statement II : A car moving towards east takes a turn and moves towards north, the speed remains unchanged. The acceleration of the car is zero.

In the light of given statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both statement I and Statement II are correct
Both statement I and statement II are incorrect
solution:
The work done in stopping the car and the truck can be written as

W=Fx=∆KE

As both the car and the truck are moving initially with the same kinetic energy, after being stopped, they will have the same change of kinetic energy.

Also, they are stopped with the same applied force.

Hence, from the above equation, it can be written that they have the same displacement, i.e., x1=x2.

Acceleration is a vector quantity. As the direction of motion of the car is different in the two given situations, the acceleration of the car will not be the same.

subject: Physics
q:
If a body looses half of its velocity on penetrating 3 cm in a wooden block, then how much will it
penetrate more before coming to rest?
question:
If a body looses half of its velocity on penetrating 3 cm in a wooden block, then how much will it
penetrate more before coming to rest?
solution:
Let the initial velocity of the body be v. Hence the final velocity = v/2
Applying $v^2=u^2-2 a s \Rightarrow\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)^2=v^2-2 \cdot a \cdot 3 \Rightarrow a=v^2 / 8$
In IInd case when the body comes to rest, final velocity = 0, initial velocity = v/2
Again, $(0)^2=\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)^2-2 \cdot \frac{v^2}{8} \cdot s ;$ or $s=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
So the extra penetration will be $1 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: A body starts moving from rest with constant acceleration covers displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> in first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math> seconds and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> seconds. The displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be made in time :
question: A body starts moving from rest with constant acceleration covers displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> in first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math> seconds and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> seconds. The displacement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be made in time :
solution:
For the displacement S1 of the body, it can be written that

S1=12a(p-1)2   ...1

And, for the displacement S2 of the body, it follows that

S2=12a(p)2   ...2

If t be the required time, it follows that

S1+S2=12at2   ...3

Equations (1), (2) and (3) imply that

(p-1)2+p2=t2⇒t=2p2-2p+1 s

subject: Physics
q: A particle starts with an initial velocity of 10.0 ms-1 along x-direction and accelerates uniformly at the rate of 2.0 m s-2. The time taken by the particle to reach the velocity of 60.0 m s-1 is _____.
question: A particle starts with an initial velocity of 10.0 ms-1 along x-direction and accelerates uniformly at the rate of 2.0 m s-2. The time taken by the particle to reach the velocity of 60.0 m s-1 is _____.
solution:
The given data is 

v=60 m s-1u=10 m s-1a=2 m s-2

Using the equation v=u+at, we get

60=10+2t⇒t=60-102⇒t=25 s

subject: Physics
q: The co-ordinates of a moving particle at any time ' $t$ ' are given by $x=\alpha t^3$ and $y=\beta t^3$. The speed of the particle at time ' $t$ ' is given by
question: The co-ordinates of a moving particle at any time ' $t$ ' are given by $x=\alpha t^3$ and $y=\beta t^3$. The speed of the particle at time ' $t$ ' is given by
solution:
$V_x=\frac{d x}{d t}=3 \alpha t^2, V_y=\frac{d y}{d t}=3 \beta t^2$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{x}^2}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{y}^2}}=3 \mathrm{t}^2 \sqrt{\alpha^2+\beta^2}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> originally at rest is subjected to a force whose direction is constant but magnitude varies with time according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>T</mi></mrow><mi>T</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> are constants. The velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> of the particle after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> is:
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> originally at rest is subjected to a force whose direction is constant but magnitude varies with time according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>T</mi></mrow><mi>T</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> are constants. The force acts only for the time interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math>. The velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> of the particle after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> is:
solution:
t=0, u=0

a=FoM-FoMT2(t-T)2=dvdt

∫0vdv=∫t=02 TFoM-FoMT2(t-T)2dt

V=FoMto2 T-FoMT2t33-t2 T+T2t02 T

V=4 FoT3M

subject: Physics
q: A clock has $75 \mathrm{~cm}, 60 \mathrm{~cm}$ long second hand and minute hand respectively. In 30 minutes duration the tip of second hand will travel $x$ distance more than the tip of minute hand. The value of $x$ in meter is nearly (Take $\pi=3.14$ ) :
question: A clock has $75 \mathrm{~cm}, 60 \mathrm{~cm}$ long second hand and minute hand respectively. In 30 minutes duration the tip of second hand will travel $x$ distance more than the tip of minute hand. The value of $x$ in meter is nearly (Take $\pi=3.14$ ) :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{x}_{\min }= & \pi \times \mathrm{r}_{\min } \\ = & \pi \times \frac{60}{100} \mathrm{~m} . \\ \mathrm{x}_{\text {second }} & =30 \times 2 \pi \times \mathrm{r}_{\text {second }} \\ & =30 \times 2 \pi \times \frac{75}{100} \\ \mathrm{x}= & \mathrm{x}_{\text {second }}-\mathrm{x}_{\min } \\ = & 139.4 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A vehicle travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then its average speed is :
question: A vehicle travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then its average speed is :
solution:
Average speed in this case is given by, 2vav=1v1+1v2

⇒2vav=13+15

⇒2vav=815

⇒vav=154=3.75 km h-1

subject: Physics
q: A car travels a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and then same distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the same direction. The average speed of the car is:
question: A car travels a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and then same distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in the same direction. The average speed of the car is:
solution:
Average velocity of car is:

vavg=Total displacementTotal time

As the car is travelling along a straight line without turning, the magnitude of total displacement is same as the distance covered. 

The time taken to cover the first half is xv1 and the time taken to cover the second half is xv2

∴   vavg = 2xxv1+xv2

⇒  vavg=2xv1v2v1x+v2x=2v1v2v1+v2

subject: Physics
q: The position of a particle as a function of time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi></math> and <math><mi>c</mi></math> are constants. When the particles zero acceleration, then its velocity will be:
question: The position of a particle as a function of time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi></math> and <math><mi>c</mi></math> are constants. When the particles zero acceleration, then its velocity will be:
solution:
x(t)=at+bt2-ct3
velocity v(t)=ddt(x)=ddtat+bt2-ct3
=a+2bt-3ct2
Acceleration =ddtv(t)=2b-6tc
acceleration=0⇒2b-6tc=0
t=b3c
∴ velocity when t=b3c,
vt=b3c=a+2bb3c-3b3c2
=a+2b23c-b23c
=a+b23c

subject: Physics
q:
From $18$ m height above the ground a ball is dropped from rest. The height above the ground at which the magnitude of velocity equal to the magnitude of acceleration (in the same set of units) due to gravity is _____ m.
(Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$ and neglect the air resistance)
question:
From $18$ m height above the ground a ball is dropped from rest. The height above the ground at which the magnitude of velocity equal to the magnitude of acceleration (in the same set of units) due to gravity is _____ m.
(Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$ and neglect the air resistance)
solution:
Let the ball fall a distance $s$ from the initial height.

Initial velocity $u = 0$

Acceleration $a = g = 10$ m/s$^2$

Given that the magnitude of velocity is equal to the magnitude of acceleration due to gravity, we have $v = 10$ m/s.

Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 + 2gs$:

$(10)^2 = 0^2 + 2 \times 10 \times s$

$100 = 20s$

$s = 5$ m

The distance fallen by the ball is $5$ m.

The height above the ground is $h = H - s$

$h = 18 - 5 = 13$ m

Answer: $13$

subject: Physics
q: A juggler throws balls vertically upwards with same initial velocity in air. When the first ball reaches its highest position, he throws the next ball. Assuming the juggler throws <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> balls per second, the maximum height the balls can reach is
question: A juggler throws balls vertically upwards with same initial velocity in air. When the first ball reaches its highest position, he throws the next ball. Assuming the juggler throws <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> balls per second, the maximum height the balls can reach is
solution:
As the juggler is throwing n balls each second and 2nd when the first is at its highest point, so the time taken by one ball to reach the highest point, t=1n s.

And as at highest point v=0.

So, from first equation of motion,

v=u+at⇒0=u-g1n⇒u=gn

Now in order to calculate height, using second equation of motion, we have:
  S=ut+12at2      ...(i)

⇒h=1n×gn-12g×1n2

     ⇒h=g2n2  

So, the balls rise up to a height of h=g2n2 m.

subject: Physics
q: Two cars are travelling towards each other at speed of $20 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ each. When the cars are $300 \mathrm{~m}$ apart, both the drivers apply brakes and the cars retard at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$. The distance between them when they come to rest is :
question: Two cars are travelling towards each other at speed of $20 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ each. When the cars are $300 \mathrm{~m}$ apart, both the drivers apply brakes and the cars retard at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$. The distance between them when they come to rest is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{BA}}\right|=40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{BA}}\right|=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \text { Apply }\left(\mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as}\right)_{\text {relative }} \\ & \mathrm{O}=(40)^2+2(-4)(\mathrm{S}) \\ & \mathrm{S}=200 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \text { Remaining distance }=300-200=100 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The velocity $(v)$ - Distance $(x)$ graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration $(a)$ versus distance $(x)$ variation of this system?
question: The velocity $(v)$ - Distance $(x)$ graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration $(a)$ versus distance $(x)$ variation of this system?
solution:
The v-x graph shows velocity decreasing linearly with distance. Using kinematic equation $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2ax$, we can establish the relationship between acceleration and position.
From the linear v-x relationship, differentiating using $a = v\frac{dv}{dx}$ yields acceleration that is linearly proportional to distance from the origin.
The a-x graph shows a straight line passing through the origin with positive slope, indicating that acceleration increases linearly with distance while remaining positive throughout the motion.

subject: Physics
q: A particle located at $x=0$ at time $t=0$, starts moving along the positive $x$-direction with a velocity ' $v$ ' that varies as $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$. The displacement of the particle varies with time as
question: A particle located at $x=0$ at time $t=0$, starts moving along the positive $x$-direction with a velocity ' $v$ ' that varies as $v=\alpha \sqrt{x}$. The displacement of the particle varies with time as
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\alpha \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}=\int \alpha \mathrm{dt} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \alpha \mathrm{t}^2$

subject: Physics
q:
The position-time graphs for two students A and B returning from the school to their homes are shown in figure.

(A) A lives closer to the school

(B) B lives closer to the school

(C) A takes lesser time to reach home

(D) A travels faster than B

(E) B travels faster than A

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
The position-time graphs for two students A and B returning from the school to their homes are shown in figure.

(A) A lives closer to the school

(B) B lives closer to the school

(C) A takes lesser time to reach home

(D) A travels faster than B

(E) B travels faster than A

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
From the position time graph the velocity is found out by the slope of the line. The slope is given by x2-x1t2-t1.

The slope of B is greater than A. Hence, the velocity of B is greater than A. As can be seen from the graph XA<XB. So A lives closer to the school and VB>VA. Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
q: Train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are running on parallel tracks in the opposite directions with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hour</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hour</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. A person is walking in train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the direction opposite to its motion with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hour</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> of this person as observed from train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be close to: (take the distance between the tracks as negligible)
question: Train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are running on parallel tracks in the opposite directions with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hour</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hour</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. A person is walking in train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the direction opposite to its motion with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hour</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> of this person as observed from train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be close to: (take the distance between the tracks as negligible)
solution:
VMA=-1.8km hr-1=-0.5 m s-1

Vman,B=Vman,A+VA,B

=Vman,A+VA-VB

=-0.5+10-(-20)

=-0.5+30

=29.5 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two cars $P$ and $Q$ are moving on a road in the same direction. Acceleration of car $P$ increases linearly with time whereas car $Q$ moves with a constant acceleration. Both cars cross each other at time $t=0$, for the first time. The maximum possible number of crossing(s) (including the crossing at $t=0)$ is ________.
question: Two cars $P$ and $Q$ are moving on a road in the same direction. Acceleration of car $P$ increases linearly with time whereas car $Q$ moves with a constant acceleration. Both cars cross each other at time $t=0$, for the first time. The maximum possible number of crossing(s) (including the crossing at $t=0)$ is ________.
solution:
$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{kt}, \mathrm{k}$ is constant $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}$ is constant $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{QP}}=\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{Q}}-\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{kt}$ as initial velocities are not mentioned in question, so will have to assume two cases. Case-I $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{OP}}$ and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{QP}}$ in same direction
Total number of crossing $=2$ Case-II $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{QP}}$ and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{QP}}$ in opposite direction
Total number of crossing $=3$

subject: Physics
q: A particle moving in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xy</mi></math>-plane experiences a velocity dependent force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub></math> are the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> components of its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is the acceleration of the particle, then which of the following statements is true for the particle ?
question: A particle moving in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xy</mi></math>-plane experiences a velocity dependent force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub></math> are the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> components of its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is the acceleration of the particle, then which of the following statements is true for the particle ?
options: kinetic energy of particle is constant in time
solution:
F→=kv→
⇒ F→‖v→
⇒ a→‖v→
⇒ v→×a→=0 always

subject: Physics
q: The relation between time ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>’ and distance ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>’ is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants. The relation between acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> and velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> is:
question: The relation between time ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>’ and distance ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>’ is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants. The relation between acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> and velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Given: t=αx2+βx 

Differentiating w.r.t time, we get

dtdx=2αx+β

⇒1v=2αx+β

Differentiating w.r.t time again, we get

⇒-1v2dvdt=2αdxdt

⇒dvdt=-2αv3as v=dxdt⇒a=dvdt=-2αv3

subject: Physics
q: A car is moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and after applying the brake it will move <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before it stops. If the same car is moving with a speed of one third the reported speed then it will stop after travelling _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance.
question: A car is moving with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and after applying the brake it will move <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before it stops. If the same car is moving with a speed of one third the reported speed then it will stop after travelling _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance.
solution:
In both cases, the final velocity is zero. Using equation of motion for constant acceleration

v2=u2+2as

⇒02-u2=-2a27    ...1

⇒02-u32=-2as'     ...2

From 1 and 2,

2a27=92as'

⇒s'=3 m

subject: Physics
q: A small steel ball is dropped into a long cylinder containing glycerine. Which one of the following is the correct representation of the velocity time graph for the transit of the ball?
question: A small steel ball is dropped into a long cylinder containing glycerine. Which one of the following is the correct representation of the velocity time graph for the transit of the ball?
solution:
Consider a small steel ball as shown below:

As shown in diagram, buoyancy force FB=F.

Applying Newton's second law, we get

mg-FB-Fv=ma

⇒ρ43πr3g-ρL43πr3g-6πηrv=mdvdt

Let 43mπR3gρ-ρL=K1 and 6πηrm=K2, then we get

⇒dvdt=K1-K2v

Integrating both sides, we get

⇒∫0vdvK1-K2v=∫0tdt

⇒-1K2lnK1-K2v0v=t

⇒lnK1-K2vK1=-K2t

⇒K1-K2v=K1e-K2t

⇒v=K1K21-e-K2t

Clearly, option B matches with the given variation of velocity with time.
